title: Economic Resilience
body: In the economic analysis of clinical research, does the stability of trial volume during global financial shocks differ between the African and European research ecosystems? This longitudinal study tracked 150,000 trials from 2000 to 2025 to evaluate the impact of the 2008 and 2020 economic downturns on research initiation. Reviewers used an economic sensitivity model and reported the year-over-year volume volatility as the primary comparative estimand for research resilience and institutional stability. The primary result showed that European trial volume remained highly resilient during economic shocks, while African research initiation exhibited significant volatility and dependency on external funding flows. Institutional fragility and the reliance on foreign lead sponsorship were identified as the primary drivers of research instability in the African hubs during these periods. These results suggest that the research divide is exacerbated by economic instability, which disproportionately affects the continuity of clinical research in low-resource settings. The findings are limited by the reliance on public
